summary:
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey (traditionally c. 8th century BC, written form) are the foundational texts of Western literature — two epic poems describing events of the Trojan War and its aftermath that shaped Greek (and subsequently Roman and European) culture, education, religion, and self-understanding for over two and a half millennia. The Iliad describes 51 days in the tenth year of the Trojan War, centered on Achilles' wrath and the death of Hector; the Odyssey follows Odysseus's ten-year return journey home. Both draw on an oral tradition stretching back centuries before their composition.

The 'Homeric Question' — whether Homer was a single author, multiple authors, or an editorial tradition — has been debated since antiquity and remains unresolved. Modern scholarship (following Milman Parry's analysis of oral-formulaic composition) holds that the poems preserve oral traditions that were gradually fixed into their surviving form. The poems were the core of Greek education from the Archaic period onward: every educated Greek knew them by memory, and they provided the shared mythological, ethical, and aesthetic vocabulary of Greek civilization. The Trojan War they described may reflect genuine Bronze Age memories (the site of Troy/Hisarlik was excavated by Heinrich Schliemann in the 1870s). The Roman poet Virgil's Aeneid was written explicitly as a continuation of the Homeric tradition.
